1:
On Ephesians 4:1 — “I therefore, the prisoner of the Lord, beseech you that ye walk worthy of the vocation wherewith ye are called” — the witnesses mark the turn to practical exhortation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the structure. Barnes: this chapter “is the commencement of the practical part of the epistle.” Robertson: “wherewith ye were called” (hes eklethete, attraction of the relative); cf. Colossians 3:12. Ellicott: being worthy of the calling shows itself in “the graces of regenerate humanity.” The Eastern Chrysostom: the teacher aims not at praise but at salvation. Wesley: imprisoned “for the sake of the gospel.”

The verse opens the second half of the letter. Paul, a prisoner for the Lord, beseeches the Ephesians to live worthily of their calling. Doctrine now passes into duty.

So the witnesses see Paul pivot from the great doctrines of the first three chapters to their practical fruit. The witnesses note the tender authority of his appeal: he beseeches — not commands harshly — and grounds the appeal in his bonds, “the prisoner of the Lord,” imprisoned for the gospel’s sake, a circumstance that lends weight and pathos to his plea. They mark the great theme of the chapters to come: to “walk worthy of the vocation” — to let the life answer to the calling, the conduct match the grace. They observe that the believer has been called by God to a high and holy estate (set forth in chapters 1–3); the fitting response is a life that adorns that calling, displaying the graces of the new humanity in Christ. They note Calvin’s observation that the remaining chapters are wholly practical exhortation, beginning with unity. The verse thus sets the keynote of the whole second half: the gospel of grace lays an obligation of holy living. Having shown what God has done, Paul now urges what the believer must be — a walk worthy of so great a calling, beginning with the lowliness and love that preserve the unity of the Spirit.

So the witnesses see Paul pivot from the great doctrines of chapters 1–3 to their practical fruit. The appeal is tender yet weighty — he beseeches, grounding it in his bonds, “the prisoner of the Lord,” imprisoned for the gospel. The great theme is to “walk worthy of the vocation” — to let the life answer the calling, the conduct match the grace. The believer has been called to a high and holy estate; the fitting response is a life that adorns it. Having shown what God has done, Paul now urges what the believer must be.
2:
On Ephesians 4:2 — “With all lowliness and meekness, with longsuffering, forbearing one another in love” — the witnesses mark the graces that preserve unity.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the virtues. Barnes: “lowliness” is humility (Philippians 2:3). Adam Clarke: meekness “the opposite to anger”; longsuffering, “long-mindedness”. The Reformed Calvin: humility is “the first step” to unity, producing meekness, which disposes us to bear with our brethren. The Puritan Henry: Christians should be “sworn enemies” to discord.

The verse names the temper that walks worthily. Lowliness, meekness, longsuffering, and mutual forbearance in love. These graces are the soil in which unity grows.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the worthy walk with the humble graces that knit believers together. The witnesses note Calvin’s ordering: humility (“lowliness”) is the first step, the lowly estimate of self that makes peace possible; from it flows meekness, the gentle, unirritable spirit that does not retaliate; and longsuffering, the patience that bears wrong over time without resentment. They mark the crown of these in “forbearing one another in love” — mutual toleration, bearing with one another’s faults and infirmities, and that not grudgingly but in love. They observe that these are precisely the virtues that preserve fellowship, for, as Calvin notes, innumerable offenses arise daily from the bitterness of human temper, which only humility and patient love can absorb. They note that pride and impatience are the great destroyers of unity, and these graces their antidote. The verse thus lays the foundation for the unity Paul will urge: a worthy walk begins not with grand deeds but with the lowly, meek, patient, loving temper that bears with the brethren. Such graces are the bond that holds the body together — preparing the explicit charge to keep the unity of the Spirit.

So the witnesses see Paul begin the worthy walk with the humble graces that knit believers together. Calvin’s ordering: humility (lowliness) is the first step; from it flows meekness, the gentle, unretaliating spirit, and longsuffering, the patience that bears wrong over time. The crown is “forbearing one another in love” — bearing with one another’s faults, and that in love. These are precisely the virtues that preserve fellowship, for innumerable offenses arise daily from human temper, which only humility and patient love can absorb. Pride and impatience are the great destroyers of unity; these graces their antidote.
3:
On Ephesians 4:3 — “Endeavouring to keep the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace” — the witnesses mark the charge to preserve unity.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson: “the unity” (henoteta, rare word); “in the bond of peace” — in Colossians 3:14 love is the bond, but “there is no peace without love.” Barnes: not one Spirit only, but “unity of affection… not… split up in factions.” Adam Clarke notes Jew and Gentile in the Ephesian church. Wesley: a unity “which is a fruit of the Spirit.”

The verse gives the explicit duty. Diligently keep the unity which the Spirit produces, held together by the bond of peace. The graces just named serve this end.

So the witnesses see Paul name the great object of the lowly, loving temper: the preservation of unity. The witnesses note the earnest word “endeavouring” — giving diligence, making it one’s serious aim; unity is not to be assumed but actively guarded. They mark that it is “the unity of the Spirit” — the oneness which the Holy Spirit creates among believers, binding them in affection and harmony; not a unity manufactured by men but received from the Spirit and to be kept. They observe the means: “in the bond of peace” — peace is the ligament that holds the members together, and love (as Colossians says) is the bond of peace; where love and peace prevail, unity is preserved. They note the Ephesian setting, where Jew and Gentile, of differing background, were liable to friction, making this charge especially apt. They observe that believers are not commanded to create unity (the Spirit has done that) but to keep it, lest it be torn by the daily offenses of life. The verse thus crowns the worthy walk’s first duty: to guard, with diligent, peaceable love, the Spirit-wrought oneness of the church — a unity Paul will now ground in the great sevenfold confession.

So the witnesses see Paul name the great object of the lowly, loving temper: the preservation of unity. The earnest word “endeavouring” means giving diligence, making it one’s serious aim; unity is to be actively guarded. It is “the unity of the Spirit” — the oneness the Holy Spirit creates, received from him and to be kept. The means is “the bond of peace” — peace the ligament, love the bond. Believers are not commanded to create unity (the Spirit has done that) but to keep it, lest it be torn by the daily offenses of life.
4:
On Ephesians 4:4 — “There is one body, and one Spirit, even as ye are called in one hope of your calling” — the witnesses mark the first three of the sevenfold unity.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the creed-like terseness. Robertson: “one body” the mystical body of Christ; “one Spirit” the Holy Spirit (neuter, but “he” not “it”); “one hope.” Ellicott: “an embryo creed.” Wesley: one body, “the universal church… one Spirit, one Lord, one God… the ever-blessed Trinity.” The Eastern Chrysostom: Paul grounds his exhortation upon things in heaven.

The verse begins the great catalogue of unities. One body, one Spirit, one hope. The oneness of the church rests on these realities.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the duty of unity in a sevenfold confession of things that are one. The witnesses note the terse, creed-like form, each clause a fundamental of the faith. The first triad: “one body” — the one mystical body of Christ, comprising all believers, Jew and Gentile, in heaven and earth; “one Spirit” — the one Holy Spirit who animates that body, the source of its life; and “one hope” — the single, common hope of heavenly glory to which all are called. They mark that these are objective realities, not aspirations: there is in truth but one church, indwelt by one Spirit, looking to one hope. They observe Chrysostom’s point that Paul grounds his exhortation on heavenly things, lifting the appeal for unity above mere expediency to the very structure of the faith. They note that since these are one, the church ought visibly to be one; division contradicts its own nature. The verse thus supplies the deepest argument for unity: believers are already one in body, Spirit, and hope, and their fellowship should reflect what they truly are. The catalogue, continued in the next verses, mounts through the Son to the Father, embracing the whole Trinity as the ground of the church’s oneness.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the duty of unity in a sevenfold confession of things that are one. The first triad: “one body” — the one mystical body of Christ; “one Spirit” — the Holy Spirit who animates it; and “one hope” — the single heavenly hope of all. They mark that these are objective realities, not aspirations: there is in truth but one church, indwelt by one Spirit, looking to one hope. Chrysostom notes Paul grounds his exhortation on heavenly things. Since these are one, the church ought visibly to be one; division contradicts its own nature.
5:
On Ephesians 4:5 — “One Lord, one faith, one baptism” — the witnesses mark the second triad of unity.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the centre. Robertson: “one Lord” the Lord Jesus Christ alone; “one faith,” one act of trust in Christ, the same for Jew or Gentile; “one baptism,” the result of baptizing. Adam Clarke: one faith, “one system of religion”; one baptism “in the name of the holy Trinity.” Ellicott: from the calling Paul passes to him who calls, the “one Lord.”

The verse gives the central triad, focused on Christ. One Lord, one faith, one baptism. The church is one in its Master, its trust, and its initiating rite.

So the witnesses see the catalogue mount from the body and Spirit to the Lord himself. The witnesses note the second triad, centred on Christ: “one Lord” — the Lord Jesus Christ alone, the one Master and Head of the church, owning no rival; “one faith” — the one trust in him by which all are saved, and the one body of gospel truth confessed, the same for Jew and Gentile; and “one baptism” — the single rite of initiation, administered in the name of the Trinity, by which believers are openly joined to Christ and his church. They mark Ellicott’s point that from the calling Paul passes naturally to him who calls and the way of coming to him — by faith, professed in baptism. They observe that these too are objective grounds of unity: one Master claims the allegiance of all, one faith unites all hearts, one baptism marks all as his. They note that to divide the church is to fracture what belongs to a single Lord, confessed by a single faith, sealed in a single baptism. The verse thus advances the argument for unity to its Christ-centred core: the church is one because its Lord, its faith, and its baptism are one — and the catalogue will rise once more, to the one God and Father over all.

So the witnesses see the catalogue mount to the Lord himself. The second triad, centred on Christ: “one Lord” — the Lord Jesus alone, the one Master owning no rival; “one faith” — the one trust in him and body of truth confessed, the same for Jew and Gentile; “one baptism” — the single rite of initiation in the Trinity’s name. From the calling Paul passes to him who calls and the way of coming — by faith, professed in baptism. To divide the church is to fracture what belongs to a single Lord, confessed by a single faith, sealed in a single baptism.
6:
On Ephesians 4:6 — “One God and Father of all, who is above all, and through all, and in you all” — the witnesses mark the climax of the unity in the Father.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the three prepositions. Robertson: “over all… through all… in all” — “by three prepositions” the universal relation of the Father; the Trinity again (Father, Son, Spirit). Adam Clarke: “above all… through all… in all.” The Reformed Calvin: this is “the main argument, from which all the rest flow” — the Father gathering us to himself.

The verse crowns the catalogue with the one God. One Father over all, through all, and in all. The unity ascends to its ultimate source.

So the witnesses see the sevenfold confession reach its summit in the one God and Father. The witnesses note that the catalogue, having named the body and Spirit and the Lord and faith, now rises to the “one God and Father of all” — the ultimate source and bond of unity, embracing the whole Trinity (Father, Son, and Spirit named across the three verses). They mark the threefold relation expressed by the prepositions: God is “above all” — supreme, sovereign over all; “through all” — pervading and working through all; and “in all” — dwelling within his people by his Spirit. They observe Calvin’s point that this is the main argument from which all the rest flow: the unity of body, Spirit, faith, and baptism exists because the one Father, extending his gracious presence to each, gathers all to himself. They note that the Fatherhood “of all” here is chiefly of all believers, the household of faith. The verse thus completes the great ground of unity: one God over, through, and in all his people, the fountain of the church’s oneness. Seven times “one” has sounded — one body, Spirit, hope, Lord, faith, baptism, God — an overwhelming argument that the church must be one because all its deepest realities are one.

So the witnesses see the sevenfold confession reach its summit in the one God and Father. The catalogue rises to the ultimate source and bond of unity, embracing the whole Trinity across the three verses. The threefold relation expressed by the prepositions: God is “above all” (sovereign), “through all” (pervading), and “in all” (indwelling by his Spirit). Calvin’s point: this is the main argument from which the rest flow — the one Father, extending his gracious presence to each, gathers all to himself. Seven times “one” has sounded: an overwhelming argument that the church must be one.
7:
On Ephesians 4:7 — “But unto every one of us is given grace according to the measure of the gift of Christ” — the witnesses mark the turn from unity to diversity of gifts.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the apportioning. Robertson: “according to the measure of the gift of Christ” — each gets the gift Christ bestows for his case (cf. Romans 12:6). Adam Clarke: grace here may signify “a particular office.” The Reformed Calvin: no member is so perfect as to need no others; “a certain proportion is allotted to each.” The Puritan Henry: each believer has “some gift of grace, for their mutual help.”

The verse turns from the church’s oneness to its variety. To each believer grace is given, in the measure Christ apportions. Unity does not mean uniformity; each has his distinct gift.

So the witnesses see Paul balance the sevenfold unity with the diversity of gifts. The witnesses note the “but”: having stressed the oneness, Paul now turns to the variety within it — for unity is not sameness. To “every one of us” grace is given — no believer is left without some gift — yet “according to the measure of the gift of Christ,” each receiving the particular endowment and proportion that Christ, the great Giver, assigns to him. They mark Calvin’s point that this very diversity serves unity: no member is complete in himself or able to supply his own needs; each depends on the others, so that the differing gifts bind the body together in mutual service. They observe that the gifts are Christ’s free bestowal, measured by his wisdom, not earned or chosen. They note Henry’s point that every believer has some gift for the mutual help of all. The verse thus introduces the second movement of the chapter: from the unity of the body to the diversity of its members’ gifts, all flowing from the one ascended Christ. The variety of grace, far from threatening unity, is the means by which the one body is built up — as the verses on Christ’s ascension and his gifts to the church will show.
8:
On Ephesians 4:8 — “Wherefore he saith, When he ascended up on high, he led captivity captive, and gave gifts unto men” — the witnesses mark the citation of the victorious ascension.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the source. Robertson: the quotation is from Psalm 68:18, a Messianic psalm of victory. Wesley: “he triumphed over all his enemies… alluding to the custom of ancient conquerors.” The Reformed Calvin candidly notes Paul departs from the psalm’s wording (“received gifts”) and defends the change. Adam Clarke ties it to the glorious ascension.

The verse grounds the giving of gifts in Christ’s triumphant ascension. Citing the Psalm, Paul shows that when Christ ascended he led captivity captive and gave gifts to men. The gifts flow from his victory.

So the witnesses see Paul cite Psalm 68 to root the church’s gifts in Christ’s conquering ascension. The witnesses note the image of the triumphant conqueror: “he led captivity captive” — like an ancient victor leading his vanquished foes in train, Christ in his ascension triumphed over sin, death, and Satan, the powers that had held men captive. They mark the famous interpretive crux: the psalm reads that the conqueror “received gifts” (or among men), while Paul writes “gave gifts unto men.” Calvin candidly faces the apparent departure and defends it: the conqueror receives spoils precisely in order to distribute them; Christ received gifts (the fulness of the Spirit) that he might bestow them on his church — so the giving is implied in the receiving, and Paul draws out the meaning rather than altering it. They observe that the gifts are thus the fruit of victory: the ascended, triumphant Christ enriches his people with the spoils of his conquest. The verse grounds the diversity of grace in the exaltation of the Lord — and its mention of ascending prompts the parenthesis on his prior descent.

So the witnesses see Paul cite Psalm 68 to root the church’s gifts in Christ’s conquering ascension. The image is the triumphant conqueror: “he led captivity captive” — like a victor leading vanquished foes in train, Christ triumphed over sin, death, and Satan. They mark the famous crux: the psalm reads “received gifts,” Paul writes “gave gifts.” Calvin defends it: the conqueror receives spoils in order to distribute them; Christ received the Spirit’s fulness that he might bestow it. The gifts are thus the fruit of victory: the ascended, triumphant Christ enriches his people with the spoils of his conquest.
9:
On Ephesians 4:9 — “(Now that he ascended, what is it but that he also descended first into the lower parts of the earth?)” — the witnesses mark the inference of the descent.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the logic and the crux. Robertson: Paul infers from “ascended” a previous “descending”; “the lower parts of the earth” debated. Ellicott: this may mean “the regions of the earth… lower than heaven,” or “the regions beneath the earth.” Adam Clarke: the descent is “his incarnation, humiliation, death, and resurrection.” Wesley: it implies descent “on the supposition of his being God.”

The verse draws an inference from the word “ascended.” That he ascended implies he first descended into the lower parts of the earth. The exalted one is the one who stooped low.

So the witnesses see Paul reason from the ascension to a prior descent. The witnesses note the logic: the very word “ascended” implies a coming down; one cannot go up who was not first below. They mark that this descent is, on the supposition of Christ’s deity, his coming down from heaven — for, as Wesley notes, the saints ascend though they never descended, but Christ’s ascent presupposes his having first come down. They observe the interpretive crux over “the lower parts of the earth”: some take it simply as the earth itself, the lower region as compared with heaven — Christ’s descent being his incarnation, humiliation, death, and burial; others read it as the regions beneath the earth, a descent into the realm of the dead. They note that the expositors generally favour the first, referring it to the depth of his self-emptying in the incarnation and death. The verse thus magnifies the One who gives the gifts: the Christ who ascended far above all is the same who first descended to the lowest depths of humiliation. His exaltation is measured by his condescension — and the next verse draws the two together to declare his purpose to fill all things.

So the witnesses see Paul reason from the ascension to a prior descent. The very word “ascended” implies a coming down; one cannot go up who was not first below. On the supposition of Christ’s deity, this descent is his coming down from heaven — for the saints ascend though they never descended (Wesley). They observe the crux over “the lower parts of the earth”: some take it as the earth itself (incarnation, death, burial); others as the regions beneath. The expositors generally favour the first. His exaltation is measured by his condescension — the One who ascended above all first descended to the lowest depths.
10:
On Ephesians 4:10 — “He that descended is the same also that ascended up far above all heavens, that he might fill all things” — the witnesses mark the identity and the purpose.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sweep. Robertson: “the one who came down (the Incarnation) is himself also the one who ascended (the Ascension)”; “far above all the heavens” (cf. Ephesians 1:21). Ellicott: “that he might fill all things” — the fulness of his grace flowing to all his members. Wesley: fill “the whole church, with his Spirit, presence, and operations.”

The verse unites the descent and ascent in one person and one purpose. He who descended is the very one who ascended above all the heavens, to fill all things. The humbled and the exalted are the same Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul join the lowest descent and the highest ascent in one Christ with one design. The witnesses note the identity: “he that descended is the same also that ascended” — not two beings but one, the same Lord who emptied himself to the depths now exalted “far above all heavens,” above every created realm, to the supreme place of glory and dominion. They mark the contrast and unity of the two movements: the depth of his humiliation answered by the height of his exaltation, both belonging to the same person. They observe the purpose: “that he might fill all things” — that the exalted Christ might pervade and fill the whole universe, and especially his church, with his presence, Spirit, grace, and operations; the fulness flowing from his glorified humanity to all his members. They note that this is why he gives gifts: the all-filling Christ pours out his grace upon the church. The verse thus completes the parenthesis on his descent and ascent: the gift-giving Lord is the one who descended to the lowest and ascended to the highest, that he might fill all things with himself. His universal fulness is the source of every gift — which Paul now enumerates in the offices given to the church.

So the witnesses see Paul join the lowest descent and the highest ascent in one Christ with one design. The identity: “he that descended is the same that ascended” — one Lord who emptied himself to the depths, now exalted “far above all heavens,” above every created realm. The depth of his humiliation is answered by the height of his exaltation. The purpose: “that he might fill all things” — that the exalted Christ might pervade the universe, and especially his church, with his presence, Spirit, and grace. This is why he gives gifts: the all-filling Christ pours out his grace upon the church.
11:
On Ephesians 4:11 — “And he gave some, apostles; and some, prophets; and some, evangelists; and some, pastors and teachers” — the witnesses mark the gift of ministers.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the four (or five) offices. Robertson: four groups; the ministers are themselves the gifts (cf. 1 Corinthians 12:28). Ellicott: “He” emphatic — the ministers are gifts from him. Wesley: apostles, “his chief ministers”; prophets, evangelists, “pastors and teachers.” The Reformed Calvin: from this variety arises unity, “as the various tones in music produce sweet melody.”

The verse lists the gifts as offices in the church. Christ gave apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors, and teachers. The ascended Lord’s chief gift to his church is its ministers.

So the witnesses see that the gifts of the ascended Christ are, supremely, the ministers he bestows. The witnesses note the striking thought, marked by Ellicott, that the men who minister Christ’s gifts are themselves his gifts to the church — living gifts of grace. They mark the orders named: “apostles” — the foundational, immediately-commissioned witnesses of the risen Christ; “prophets” — those who spoke by revelation; “evangelists” — preachers who carried the gospel to new fields; and “pastors and teachers” — the settled shepherds who feed and instruct the flock. They observe the question whether “pastors and teachers” name one office or two; many take them as a single class (the shepherd who also teaches), though some distinguish them. They note Calvin’s beautiful point that from this very diversity of offices springs the church’s unity, as varied musical tones blend into one melody. They observe that the first two orders were foundational and temporary, the latter perpetual. The verse thus shows how the all-filling Christ builds his church: by giving it a variety of ministers, each with his gift, for the common good — whose great purpose Paul now states.

So the witnesses see that the gifts of the ascended Christ are, supremely, the ministers he bestows — the men who minister his gifts being themselves his gifts (Ellicott). The orders: “apostles” (foundational witnesses), “prophets” (revelation), “evangelists” (gospel pioneers), and “pastors and teachers” (settled shepherds). They note the question whether the last name one office or two; many take them as a single class. Calvin’s beautiful point: from this very diversity springs unity, as varied musical tones blend into one melody. The first orders were foundational and temporary, the latter perpetual.
12:
On Ephesians 4:12 — “For the perfecting of the saints, for the work of the ministry, for the edifying of the body of Christ” — the witnesses mark the purpose of the offices.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “perfecting” (katartismon), “the mending (repair) of the saints,” equipping; “the building up” (oikodomen). Adam Clarke: “complete instruction, purification, and union.” Ellicott notes the prepositions distinguish the clauses. The Puritan Henry: the office of ministers, the aim of the saints, the way of growing.

The verse gives the threefold purpose of the ministry. The offices are for equipping the saints, for the work of service, and for building up the body of Christ. The gifts aim at the maturing of the whole church.

So the witnesses see the great purpose for which Christ gave the ministers. The witnesses note the key word “perfecting” — katartismos, the mending, fitting, or equipping; the same root used of setting a bone or mending a net — so the ministers are given to equip and fit the saints for their calling. They mark the threefold aim: “for the perfecting of the saints” — that believers be furnished and matured; “for the work of the ministry” or service — that they be enabled to do the work; and “for the edifying of the body of Christ” — the building up of the whole church. They observe a significant reading the grammar suggests: the ministers equip the saints, so that the saints themselves do the work of ministry, and thereby the body is built up — the whole church, not the clergy alone, sharing in the work. They note Henry’s outline: the office of ministers, the aim of the saints, the way of growth. The verse thus reveals the design of the gifts: not to make the church passive under its ministers, but to equip every saint for service, that the body may grow. The pastors and teachers exist to build up the whole, toward the full maturity Paul now describes.

So the witnesses see the great purpose for which Christ gave the ministers. The key word “perfecting” — equipping, fitting, mending, as setting a bone — so the ministers are given to equip the saints. The threefold aim: “perfecting of the saints” (furnishing and maturing them), “the work of the ministry” (enabling the work), and “the edifying of the body” (building up the whole). The grammar suggests the ministers equip the saints so that the saints do the work, and the body is built up — the whole church, not the clergy alone, sharing the work. The gifts exist to build up the whole.
13:
On Ephesians 4:13 — “Till we all come in the unity of the faith, and of the knowledge of the Son of God, unto a perfect man, unto the measure of the stature of the fulness of Christ” — the witnesses mark the goal of growth.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the destination. Robertson: “till we all attain” (katantesomen), come down to the goal; “the whole” including every individual. Ellicott: “arrive at the unity of the faith.” Adam Clarke: a “true understanding of the mystery of the incarnation.” Wesley: “a perfect man — a state of spiritual manhood.” The Reformed Calvin: the ministry continues to the end.

The verse states the goal of the ministry’s work. It continues till all attain unity of faith and knowledge of the Son of God, full spiritual maturity, the measure of Christ’s fulness. The aim is nothing less than the perfecting of all in Christ.

So the witnesses see the lofty goal toward which the gifts and offices labour. The witnesses note that the ministry is given “till we all come” — a work that continues, as Calvin observes, to the very end, not for a day but throughout the church’s pilgrimage, and embracing every individual. They mark the threefold goal: “the unity of the faith, and of the knowledge of the Son of God” — that all attain to one settled faith and full experiential knowledge of Christ; “unto a perfect man” — spiritual manhood, full maturity, as opposed to the childhood of the next verse; and “unto the measure of the stature of the fulness of Christ” — the standard being nothing less than the complete likeness of Christ himself, the church grown up into his fulness. They observe that this is both a corporate and an individual goal: the whole body, and each member, growing toward Christlike completeness. They note the grandeur of the aim — not mere instruction but transformation into the image of the Son. The verse thus sets the horizon of all ministry: the bringing of the whole church to unity, knowledge, and the full stature of Christ — a maturity that leaves behind the instability of spiritual childhood.
14:
On Ephesians 4:14 — “That we henceforth be no more children, tossed to and fro, and carried about with every wind of doctrine, by the sleight of men” — the witnesses mark the instability to be left behind.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figures. Robertson: “no longer children” (nepioi), content to remain babes (Hebrews 5:13); “tossed to and fro” (klydonizomenoi), like waves. Adam Clarke: children opposed to the perfect man. Wesley: “by the sleight of men — by their cogging the dice,” the original implying trickery.

The verse describes the childhood maturity overcomes. We are to be no more children, tossed by waves and blown about by every doctrinal wind, deceived by crafty men. Maturity brings stability.

So the witnesses see the negative side of the goal: deliverance from the instability of spiritual childhood. The witnesses note that “children” here means the immature, the undiscerning, who lack settled judgment — some Christians, Robertson observes, content to remain babes and never grow. They mark the vivid figures of instability: “tossed to and fro” like a ship on restless waves, and “carried about with every wind of doctrine” — blown this way and that by each new teaching, having no anchor of established conviction. They observe the cause: “the sleight of men” — the trickery, cunning, and craftiness of deceivers, the word (Wesley notes) implying the loaded dice of cheats. They note that such immaturity makes the believer the easy prey of every charlatan and false teacher. The verse thus shows what spiritual maturity rescues us from: the rudderless, gullible state that is at the mercy of error and deceit. The aim of the ministry (v. 13) is precisely to bring believers past this childhood into stable, discerning manhood — no longer blown about, but grounded in the truth, which Paul now sets in its positive form: speaking the truth in love and growing up into Christ.

So the witnesses see the negative side of the goal: deliverance from the instability of spiritual childhood. “Children” here means the immature, undiscerning, lacking settled judgment — some content to remain babes. The vivid figures: “tossed to and fro” like a ship on restless waves, “carried about with every wind of doctrine,” blown this way and that with no anchor of conviction. The cause: “the sleight of men” — the trickery of deceivers, the loaded dice of cheats (Wesley). Such immaturity makes the believer easy prey for every false teacher; maturity rescues into stable, discerning manhood.
15:
On Ephesians 4:15 — “But speaking the truth in love, may grow up into him in all things, which is the head, even Christ” — the witnesses mark the way of growth.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the key phrase. Robertson: “in love” — “if truth were always spoken only in love!”; “may grow into him,” Christ the Head. Barnes: the simple, unvarnished truth, to avoid error. Adam Clarke: the truth “must be preached in love… scolding” profits not. The Reformed Calvin notes the word means embracing and adhering to truth, joined with love.

The verse gives the positive path to maturity. By holding and speaking the truth in love, we grow up in everything into Christ, the Head. Truth and love together build the body.

So the witnesses see the positive remedy for childish instability: truth held and spoken in love. The witnesses note the balance of the famous phrase: “the truth” — the simple, sound doctrine of the gospel, embraced and adhered to (Calvin), the safeguard against being blown about; and “in love” — for truth without love hardens and repels, while love without truth flatters and deceives. They mark Robertson’s wistful note — would that truth were always spoken only in love — and Clarke’s that scolding profits nothing; the truth must be conveyed in the spirit of love. They observe the goal of this truthful, loving life: “may grow up into him in all things, which is the head, even Christ” — the whole man, in every part, advancing toward Christlikeness, for Christ is the Head into whom the body grows. They note that maturity is thus growth into Christ, not merely the accumulation of knowledge. The verse thus sets the way to the maturity of verse 13: not the restless tossing of childhood, but a steady growing-up into the Head, by holding the truth in a loving spirit. It prepares the final picture of the whole body, knit to its Head, building itself up in love.

So the witnesses see the positive remedy for childish instability: truth held and spoken in love. The balance: “the truth” — sound doctrine embraced and adhered to, the safeguard against being blown about; and “in love” — for truth without love hardens, while love without truth deceives. Robertson’s wistful note: would that truth were always spoken only in love. The goal: “grow up into him in all things, which is the head, even Christ” — the whole man advancing toward Christlikeness, for Christ is the Head into whom the body grows. Maturity is growth into Christ, not merely accumulated knowledge.
16:
On Ephesians 4:16 — “From whom the whole body fitly joined together and compacted by that which every joint supplieth… maketh increase of the body unto the edifying of itself in love” — the witnesses mark the body growing from its Head.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the anatomy. Robertson: “from which” (Christ the source); “fitly framed” (synarmologoumenon, cf. Ephesians 2:21); “through every joint of the supply.” Adam Clarke quotes Macknight on the body’s members. The Reformed Calvin: all vigour flows from the Head, as sap from the root. Wesley: each part “fitted for and adapted to” the whole.

The verse completes the figure of the growing body. From Christ the Head, the whole body, fitly joined and supplied through every joint, grows and builds itself up in love. Each member contributes to the increase of all.

So the witnesses see Paul crown the passage with the image of the body growing from its Head. The witnesses note the source: “from whom” — from Christ the Head flows all the life, energy, and increase of the body, as sap rises from the root through the whole tree (Calvin). They mark the structure: the body “fitly joined together and compacted” — every member framed and bonded into its place, like a well-built frame; and “by that which every joint supplieth” — each part contributing its share, so that the whole is knit together by the mutual supply of all. They observe the result: the body “maketh increase… unto the edifying of itself in love” — growing and building itself up, each member working for the good of all, and the whole done “in love.” They note the double truth: all increase derives from Christ the Head, yet it comes through the mutual ministry of the members; the church grows neither by the clergy alone nor by independent units, but by the loving, fitted cooperation of all under Christ. The verse thus completes the second movement: the gifts of the ascended Christ build a body that, joined to its Head and knit together in love, grows up into him — before Paul turns to the new life that such members must lead.
17:
On Ephesians 4:17 — “This I say therefore, and testify in the Lord, that ye henceforth walk not as other Gentiles walk, in the vanity of their mind” — the witnesses mark the turn to holiness of life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the solemn charge. Robertson: “in vanity of their mind” (mataioteti, emptiness, Romans 8:20). Wesley: “vanity of mind… the root of all evil walking” (Romans 1:21). The Puritan Henry: an exhortation to purity and holiness. The Eastern Chrysostom: the teacher builds up by alarming as well as counseling.

The verse begins the call to a holy walk, distinct from the heathen. Paul solemnly charges them not to live as the Gentiles do, in the emptiness of their minds. The new life must differ from the old.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from unity and growth to the holiness that must mark the new humanity. The witnesses note the solemnity: “I say… and testify in the Lord” — a weighty, authoritative charge, given in the Lord’s name. They mark the demand: “walk not as other Gentiles walk” — the converted are no longer to live like the unconverted heathen from whom they were called. They observe the root of pagan life named first: “the vanity of their mind” — the emptiness, futility, and aimlessness of a mind that has lost the knowledge of the true God; this, Wesley says, is the root of all evil walking. They note that Paul traces the corruption of the heathen life not merely to outward acts but to a darkened, empty mind — the inward source from which the outward sins flow. They observe Chrysostom’s point that the teacher must alarm as well as instruct. The verse thus opens the contrast between the old and the new walk: the believer, possessing the truth and growing into Christ, must not relapse into the vain, godless manner of life that characterizes those without God — whose deepening darkness and corruption Paul will now describe.

So the witnesses see Paul turn from unity and growth to the holiness that must mark the new humanity. The solemnity: “I say… and testify in the Lord” — a weighty charge in the Lord’s name. The demand: “walk not as other Gentiles walk” — the converted no longer living like the unconverted. The root of pagan life is named first: “the vanity of their mind” — the emptiness and futility of a mind that has lost the knowledge of God; this, Wesley says, is the root of all evil walking. Paul traces the corruption not merely to outward acts but to a darkened, empty mind.
18:
On Ephesians 4:18 — “Having the understanding darkened, being alienated from the life of God through the ignorance that is in them, because of the blindness of their heart” — the witnesses mark the darkness of the heathen mind.

The grammatical-historical commentators trace the cause. Robertson: “being darkened” (eskotomenoi) in their understanding (dianoia, including the emotions). Ellicott: “the life of God” is “the life which God lives… and gives.” Adam Clarke: the heart “naturally dark, became more so by habitual transgression.” Wesley: “utter strangers to the divine, the spiritual life… through the hardness of their hearts.”

The verse describes the inner darkness behind heathen vanity. Their understanding is darkened, they are estranged from God’s life through ignorance and hardness of heart. The mind’s emptiness deepens into spiritual death.

So the witnesses see Paul trace the heathen condition to its dark inward roots. The witnesses note the chain of corruption: the “understanding darkened” — the mind clouded and blind in spiritual things, unable to perceive God or his truth; and so “alienated from the life of God” — estranged, cut off from the divine, spiritual life that God lives and imparts to his own. They mark the cause: “through the ignorance that is in them, because of the blindness of their heart” — not a mere lack of information, but a deep, willful ignorance springing from a hardened, blind heart. They observe Clarke’s point that the heart, naturally dark, grows darker through habitual sin; the blindness is moral, not merely intellectual. They note that this alienation from God’s life is the heart of the heathen tragedy: not poverty or ignorance of fact, but separation from the very life of God. The verse thus deepens the diagnosis of verse 17: the vanity of the heathen mind is no surface fault but a darkened understanding and a hardened heart, cutting men off from God — a callousness that, as the next verse shows, gives them over to unbridled sin.

So the witnesses see Paul trace the heathen condition to its dark inward roots. The chain of corruption: the “understanding darkened” — the mind blind in spiritual things; and so “alienated from the life of God” — cut off from the divine life God lives and imparts. The cause: “through the ignorance that is in them, because of the blindness of their heart” — a deep, willful ignorance from a hardened heart. Clarke’s point: the heart, naturally dark, grows darker through habitual sin; the blindness is moral, not merely intellectual. Separation from God’s very life is the heart of the heathen tragedy.
19:
On Ephesians 4:19 — “Who being past feeling have given themselves over unto lasciviousness, to work all uncleanness with greediness” — the witnesses mark the descent into unbridled sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the callousness. Robertson: “past feeling” (apelgekotes), to cease to feel pain; “lasciviousness” (aselgeia), unbridled lust (Galatians 5:19); “all uncleanness with greediness.” Wesley: “past feeling pain… pain urges to seek a remedy.” Ellicott: they have “lost the capacity of pain… the moral pain.”

The verse reaches the depth of heathen corruption. Past all feeling, they give themselves over to sensuality, working every uncleanness greedily. Conscience deadened, they plunge into sin without restraint.

So the witnesses see the final stage of the heathen descent: conscience seared, sin unrestrained. The witnesses note the dreadful phrase “past feeling” — the conscience cauterized, the moral sense deadened, so that they no longer feel the pain or shame of sin. They mark Wesley’s acute observation that the word means past feeling pain, and that pain is what urges the sick to seek a cure; having lost it, they no longer seek remedy or reformation. They observe the result of this callousness: “given themselves over” — willingly, of their own accord, surrendering — “unto lasciviousness,” unbridled sensuality, “to work all uncleanness with greediness” — pursuing every kind of impurity insatiably, as a trade, with eager appetite. They note the awful progression of the three verses: vanity of mind, darkened understanding and alienation, and now seared conscience and greedy abandonment to sin — the full anatomy of life without God. The verse thus completes the dark portrait of the old, heathen walk from which the believer has been delivered: a self-given slavery to lust, beyond feeling, beyond restraint. Against this black background Paul will set the new life learned in Christ.

So the witnesses see the final stage of the heathen descent: conscience seared, sin unrestrained. The dreadful phrase “past feeling” — the conscience cauterized, the moral sense deadened. Wesley’s acute note: the word means past feeling pain, and pain is what urges the sick to seek a cure; having lost it, they no longer seek reformation. The result: “given themselves over” — willingly — “unto lasciviousness, to work all uncleanness with greediness,” pursuing every impurity insatiably. The awful progression — vanity, darkness, seared conscience — is the full anatomy of life without God.
20:
On Ephesians 4:20 — “But ye have not so learned Christ” — the witnesses mark the contrast of the Christian.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the sharp contrast. Robertson: “in sharp contrast to pagan life” (houtos); “ye did not so learn Christ.” Ellicott: to “learn Christ” is a unique phrase, near to “know Christ.” Adam Clarke: ye have received the doctrines and Spirit of Christ, and so are saved from such dispositions. Wesley: “ye cannot act thus, now ye know him.”

The verse turns sharply to the believer. But you did not so learn Christ. The Christian life stands in stark contrast to the heathen corruption just described.

So the witnesses see Paul break off the dark portrait with an emphatic contrast. The witnesses note the abruptness: “but ye have not so learned Christ” — a sharp turn from the heathen vileness to the wholly different thing the Ephesians had been taught. They mark the unusual phrase “learned Christ” — not merely learned about Christ, but learned Christ himself, as one learns a person; to be taught Christ is to receive him, his doctrine, his Spirit, his very self, which transforms the life. They observe that this learning is incompatible with the old corruption: the Christian, having learned Christ, “cannot act thus” (Wesley), for the gospel allows no such sin. They note that the contrast is not merely between two moralities but between two masters and two natures: the heathen walk in the vanity of their mind; the Christian has learned Christ, and so is governed by an altogether different principle. The verse thus pivots from the old life to the new: whatever the Gentiles do, the believer has been schooled in Christ, and that schooling forbids the life of lust. It introduces the explicit teaching the Ephesians received — to put off the old man and put on the new.

So the witnesses see Paul break off the dark portrait with an emphatic contrast. The abruptness: “but ye have not so learned Christ” — a sharp turn from heathen vileness to the wholly different thing the Ephesians were taught. The unusual phrase “learned Christ” — not merely learned about Christ, but learned Christ himself, as one learns a person; to be taught Christ is to receive his doctrine, Spirit, and self. This learning is incompatible with the old corruption: the Christian “cannot act thus” (Wesley). The contrast is between two masters and two natures — the heathen walk in vanity, the Christian governed by Christ.
21:
On Ephesians 4:21 — “If so be that ye have heard him, and have been taught by him, as the truth is in Jesus” — the witnesses mark the schooling in Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the assumed condition. Robertson: “if so be that” (ei ge), first-class condition, assumed true; “heard him… taught in him… as truth is in Jesus.” Ellicott: “rhetorical doubt… strong affirmation”; the first means of learning, hearing. The Reformed Calvin: the doctrine was “faithfully delivered and explained,” a reproof of superficial knowledge.

The verse describes how they were taught. If indeed they heard Christ and were taught in him, as the truth is in Jesus. Their instruction was a real schooling in the truth embodied in Christ.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the new walk in the genuine instruction the Ephesians received in Christ. The witnesses note that “if so be that” expresses not real doubt but strong affirmation — a confident “since indeed ye heard him.” They mark the three stages: they “heard him” — heard Christ preached, and through the preaching heard Christ himself; were “taught by him” (or in him) — instructed not merely in doctrines but in union with Christ; and that “as the truth is in Jesus” — the teaching being the very truth that resides and is embodied in the historical Jesus. They observe Calvin’s point that this implies a faithful, thorough delivery of the doctrine, and a reproof of any superficial, nominal knowledge that does not transform. They note that to be “taught in him as the truth is in Jesus” is to have learned reality, not theory — the truth as it actually is in the living Christ. The verse thus reinforces verse 20: the Ephesians’ Christian instruction was a real, thorough schooling in the truth embodied in Jesus — the ground on which Paul now spells out the content of that teaching: putting off the old man, being renewed, putting on the new.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the new walk in the genuine instruction the Ephesians received. “If so be that” expresses not real doubt but strong affirmation. The three stages: they “heard him” (Christ preached and himself heard), were “taught by him” (in union with Christ), and that “as the truth is in Jesus” (the truth embodied in the historical Jesus). Calvin’s point: this implies a faithful, thorough delivery, a reproof of superficial knowledge that does not transform. To be taught “as the truth is in Jesus” is to have learned reality, not theory — the truth as it actually is in the living Christ.
22:
On Ephesians 4:22 — “That ye put off concerning the former conversation the old man, which is corrupt according to the deceitful lusts” — the witnesses mark the putting off of the old man.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the metaphor. Robertson: “put away” (apothesthai), the figure of putting off clothing; “the old man” (cf. Colossians 3:9; Romans 6:6). Adam Clarke: the corrupt former nature. The Reformed Calvin: he demands repentance — “to lay aside the old man,” using the metaphor of garments. Wesley: the old man, “the whole body of sin… deceitful, promising the happiness they cannot give.”

The verse gives the first part of the teaching: put off the old self. The old man, the corrupt former nature ruined by deceitful lusts, is to be laid aside like a worn garment. The new walk begins with renunciation.

So the witnesses see the content of the Christian schooling begin with the putting off of the old self. The witnesses note the metaphor of clothing: the “old man” — the whole corrupt nature inherited from Adam, the body of sin that belonged to the “former conversation,” the old manner of life — is to be “put off,” laid aside and renounced like a filthy, worn-out garment. They mark its character: “corrupt according to the deceitful lusts” — the old man is being destroyed, growing ever more corrupt, under the power of lusts that deceive, promising (Wesley says) a happiness they cannot give and leading to ruin. They observe Calvin’s point that this is the demand for repentance, the first part of the new life, the mortification of sin. They note that the putting off is decisive and continual: the believer is to have done with the old nature and its deceiving desires. The verse thus gives the negative side of the new walk — the renunciation of the corrupt old self — which is inseparable from the renewal and the putting on that follow. The Christian life begins with stripping off the deceitful, decaying garment of the old man.

So the witnesses see the content of the Christian schooling begin with the putting off of the old self. The metaphor of clothing: the “old man” — the whole corrupt Adamic nature of the “former conversation” — is to be laid aside like a filthy, worn-out garment. Its character: “corrupt according to the deceitful lusts” — decaying under lusts that deceive, promising a happiness they cannot give (Wesley). Calvin’s point: this is the demand for repentance, the mortification of sin. The Christian life begins with stripping off the deceitful, decaying garment of the old man.
23:
On Ephesians 4:23 — “And be renewed in the spirit of your mind” — the witnesses mark the inward renewal.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the renewing. Robertson: “be renewed” (ananeousthai), to make new (young) again (cf. Colossians 3:10); the spirit of the mind, the seat of thinking. Adam Clarke: “all its faculties… thoroughly… renewed.” Ellicott: “to be made young again.” The Reformed Calvin: to live in the Spirit of Christ, beginning with the mind.

The verse gives the middle term: inward renewal. Between putting off and putting on stands the renewing of the very spirit of the mind. The change reaches the inmost springs of thought.

So the witnesses see the inward transformation that lies between the putting off and the putting on. The witnesses note the seat of the renewal: “the spirit of your mind” — the inmost principle of the understanding and will, the deepest spring of thought and disposition. They mark the word “renewed” — to be made new, even made young again, the decay of the old man giving way to the freshness of a regenerated nature. They observe that this renewal is the work of the Holy Spirit, who renovates all the faculties of the mind, not merely curbing outward acts but transforming the inner man at its source. They note Calvin’s point that the new life is to live in the Spirit of Christ, and that this begins with the mind, the part that governs the rest. They observe the placement of this clause: it is the hinge between the negative (putting off) and the positive (putting on) — for outward change without inward renewal is hollow, and the new man can only be put on where the mind itself has been made new. The verse thus locates the heart of sanctification in the renewing of the inner man: not reformation of conduct only, but a deep, Spirit-wrought making-new of the mind’s very spirit, from which the new life flows.

So the witnesses see the inward transformation between the putting off and the putting on. The seat of the renewal: “the spirit of your mind” — the inmost principle of understanding and will. The word “renewed” means made new, even made young again, the decay of the old man giving way to the freshness of a regenerated nature. This is the Spirit’s work, renovating all the faculties at their source. The placement is the hinge between the negative and positive — outward change without inward renewal is hollow. Sanctification’s heart is a deep, Spirit-wrought making-new of the mind’s very spirit.
24:
On Ephesians 4:24 — “And that ye put on the new man, which after God is created in righteousness and true holiness” — the witnesses mark the putting on of the new man.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the new creation. Robertson: “put on” (endysasthai) the “brand-new man” (kainon); “after God,” the pattern God (2 Corinthians 5:17). Adam Clarke: “a new creation… in righteousness and true holiness.” The Reformed Calvin: the second creation, by the grace of Christ. The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem links it to the new birth.

The verse completes the threefold change: put on the new man. The new self, created after God’s likeness in righteousness and true holiness, is to be put on like a garment. The image of God is restored.

So the witnesses see the positive completion of the change: the putting on of the new man. The witnesses note the matching metaphor: as the old man was put off like a filthy garment, so the “new man” is to be “put on” — the brand-new nature created in regeneration, to be worn and displayed in the life. They mark its origin and pattern: “which after God is created” — created by God, after God’s own likeness, the image of God restored in the renewed soul, a new creation (as Paul says elsewhere, “if any man be in Christ, he is a new creature”). They observe its character: “in righteousness and true holiness” — the two great marks of the divine image, righteousness toward men and holiness toward God, and that “true” holiness, real and not counterfeit. They note Calvin’s point that this is the second creation, wrought by the grace of Christ, answering to the first creation of man in God’s image. They observe that the three clauses together (vv. 22–24) give the whole of sanctification: put off the old, be renewed within, put on the new. The verse thus crowns the change: the believer is to clothe himself with the new, God-created self, bearing the restored image of God in righteousness and true holiness — the new humanity that the particular duties following will display.
25:
On Ephesians 4:25 — “Wherefore putting away lying, speak every man truth with his neighbour: for we are members one of another” — the witnesses mark the first particular duty.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the application. Robertson: “putting away” (apothemenoi) lying, truth in direct contrast (cf. Colossians 3:9). Ellicott: “truth is the first condition of mutual confidence… the basis of all unity.” The Eastern Chrysostom expounds the “old man” in detail. The Puritan Henry: God’s people “dare not lie.”

The verse begins the particular duties of the new man, starting with truthfulness. Put away lying and speak truth, for believers are members of one another. The new nature shows itself first in honesty.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the general renewal to its first concrete expression: truthfulness. The witnesses note the “wherefore” — because you have put on the new man, therefore put away lying; the particular duties flow from the new nature. They mark the contrast: “putting away lying” (a garment of the old man) and “speak every man truth with his neighbour” — the plain, honest truth replacing falsehood. They observe the striking reason: “for we are members one of another” — believers belong to one body, and as the members of a physical body do not deceive one another (the eye does not lie to the hand), so members of Christ’s body must deal truthfully. They note Ellicott’s point that truth is the first condition of the mutual confidence on which all unity rests; lying tears apart the body. They observe that, in the heathen world, lying was reckoned a small thing, but the new man abhors it. The verse thus gives the first duty of the renewed life: honesty, grounded not merely in a rule but in the organic unity of the body of Christ. Because believers are members one of another, truth among them is as natural and necessary as truth between the members of one’s own body.

So the witnesses see Paul move from the general renewal to its first concrete expression: truthfulness. The “wherefore” — because you have put on the new man, therefore put away lying; the duties flow from the new nature. The contrast: lying put away, “truth” spoken. The striking reason: “for we are members one of another” — as the members of a body do not deceive one another, so members of Christ’s body must deal truthfully. Ellicott’s point: truth is the first condition of the mutual confidence on which all unity rests. Honesty is grounded not merely in a rule but in the organic unity of the body of Christ.
26:
On Ephesians 4:26 — “Be ye angry, and sin not: let not the sun go down upon your wrath” — the witnesses mark the regulation of anger.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the permissive sense. Robertson: “permissive imperative, not a command to be angry” — a prohibition against sinning in anger (quoting Psalm 4:4). Adam Clarke: “if ye be angry, do not sin.” Ellicott: anger itself is not sin, for Christ felt it (Mark 3:5). Wesley: “anger at sin is not evil; but we should feel only pity to the sinner.”

The verse regulates rather than forbids anger. Be angry without sinning; let not the sun set on your wrath. Anger is to be brief, righteous, and quickly laid aside.

So the witnesses see Paul govern the passion of anger rather than forbid it outright. The witnesses note the permissive sense the expositors draw: not a command to be angry, but a concession — “if you are angry, sin not” — for anger itself is not necessarily sinful; Christ himself felt holy anger at the hardness of men’s hearts, and God’s anger burns against sin. They mark Wesley’s careful balance: anger at sin is lawful, but it must be free from sin — not mingled with malice, pride, or revenge, and directed at the fault while pitying the offender. They observe the safeguard against anger turning sinful: “let not the sun go down upon your wrath” — anger must not be nursed or prolonged; reconcile quickly, before the day ends, lest a passing emotion harden into settled resentment. They note that the peril lies not in the first stir of anger but in cherishing it. The verse thus teaches the right government of a dangerous passion: anger may be felt, even ought to be felt against evil, but it must be kept from sin and never allowed to fester — for prolonged wrath, as the next verse warns, gives the devil his opportunity.

So the witnesses see Paul govern the passion of anger rather than forbid it. The permissive sense: not a command to be angry, but “if you are angry, sin not” — for anger itself is not necessarily sinful; Christ felt holy anger, and God’s anger burns against sin. Wesley’s balance: anger at sin is lawful, but free from malice, directed at the fault while pitying the offender. The safeguard: “let not the sun go down upon your wrath” — anger must not be nursed; reconcile quickly, lest a passing emotion harden into settled resentment. The peril lies not in the first stir but in cherishing it.
27:
On Ephesians 4:27 — “Neither give place to the devil” — the witnesses mark the warning against the tempter.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “give place” (topon) to the devil — prohibition (cf. Romans 12:19). Adam Clarke notes diabolos can mean “calumniator,” but here the tempter. Barnes: do not yield to Satan’s suggestions to cherish anger. The Reformed Calvin: Paul guards against “allowing Satan to take possession” through anger.

The verse warns against giving the devil an opening. Give no place to the devil. Cherished anger is the very foothold Satan seeks.

So the witnesses see Paul connect prolonged anger with the devil’s opportunity. The witnesses note the figure: to “give place” is to give room, scope, a foothold — an opening through which the enemy may enter. They mark the connection with the foregoing verse: nursed, festering anger is precisely the place the devil seeks; the resentment that the sun goes down upon becomes Satan’s entry-point, turning a passing emotion into rooted bitterness, malice, and division. They observe that while some take “the devil” here as a human slanderer, the expositors generally and rightly understand it of Satan, the great tempter and accuser, who exploits the believer’s unguarded passions. They note Calvin’s point that Paul guards against letting Satan take possession through anger. They observe the broader principle: every cherished sin gives the devil a foothold; the believer is to leave him no room. The verse thus drives home the warning of verse 26: do not let anger linger, for to do so is to hand the devil an opening into the heart and the fellowship. The new man, vigilant against the tempter, refuses him any place — and turns, as the next verses show, to positive virtues of honest labour and edifying speech.

So the witnesses see Paul connect prolonged anger with the devil’s opportunity. To “give place” is to give room, a foothold — an opening through which the enemy may enter. The connection with the foregoing verse: nursed, festering anger is precisely the place the devil seeks, turning a passing emotion into rooted bitterness and division. The expositors generally understand “the devil” of Satan, the tempter, who exploits unguarded passions. Calvin’s point: Paul guards against letting Satan take possession through anger. Every cherished sin gives the devil a foothold; the new man leaves him no room.
28:
On Ephesians 4:28 — “Let him that stole steal no more: but rather let him labour, working with his hands the thing which is good, that he may have to give to him that needeth” — the witnesses mark honest labour replacing theft.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the positive turn. Robertson: “steal no more” (cease stealing); “the good thing” with his hands; even unemployment is no excuse (2 Thessalonians 3:10). Adam Clarke: against the rabbinic toleration of theft if a portion went to the poor. Wesley: labour “lest idleness lead him to steal again… that he may have to give.”

The verse replaces theft with honest, generous labour. Let the thief steal no more, but work with his hands at good labour, so as to have something to give the needy. The change is from taking to giving.

So the witnesses see Paul not merely forbid theft but transform the thief into a generous worker. The witnesses note the threefold movement: first, the prohibition — “let him that stole steal no more,” an end to dishonest taking; second, the positive substitute — “let him labour, working with his hands the thing which is good,” honest, useful toil replacing idleness and pilfering, for idleness, as Wesley notes, is the road back to stealing; and third, the generous end — “that he may have to give to him that needeth,” so that the former thief, who once took from others, now earns in order to give to others. They mark the beauty of the complete reversal: the hands that stole now work, and work not merely to support self but to relieve the poor. They observe Clarke’s note that this corrects even the lax view that tolerated theft if some went to charity; the new man earns honestly and gives freely. They note the principle that whoever has sinned in a thing should zealously practise the opposite virtue. The verse thus shows the new man’s ethic: not bare abstinence from evil, but the replacing of vice with its contrary virtue — theft transformed into honest labour and open-handed generosity.

So the witnesses see Paul not merely forbid theft but transform the thief into a generous worker. The threefold movement: the prohibition — “steal no more”; the positive substitute — “let him labour, working with his hands the thing which is good,” for idleness is the road back to stealing (Wesley); and the generous end — “that he may have to give to him that needeth.” The complete reversal is beautiful: the hands that stole now work, and work not merely to support self but to relieve the poor. The new man’s ethic is not bare abstinence from evil but the replacing of vice with its contrary virtue.
29:
On Ephesians 4:29 — “Let no corrupt communication proceed out of your mouth, but that which is good to the use of edifying, that it may minister grace unto the hearers” — the witnesses mark the rule for speech.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the word. Robertson: “corrupt” (sapros), rotten, putrid like bad fruit (Matthew 7:17); “for the build-up of the need.” Adam Clarke: useless, putrid, obscene speech. The Puritan Henry: filthy words corrupt speaker and hearers. Wesley: that it “may minister grace… convey more grace into their hearts.”

The verse governs the tongue. Let no rotten speech come out, but only what is good for edifying and ministers grace to the hearers. Speech is to build up, not corrupt.

So the witnesses see Paul regulate the speech of the new man. The witnesses note the strong word “corrupt” — rotten, putrid, like decayed fruit or fish — covering foul, obscene, useless, and harmful talk, all that defiles. They mark the prohibition: none of this is to “proceed out of your mouth” — for, as Henry notes, filthy words spring from corruption in the speaker and corrupt the hearers. They observe the positive standard, which is high: speech is to be “good to the use of edifying” — helpful for building up, suited to the need of the moment — “that it may minister grace unto the hearers,” actually conveying spiritual benefit, even grace, to those who hear. They note that the test of the tongue is not merely the absence of evil but the presence of good: does this word build up, meet a need, do the hearer good? They observe the high calling implied — that ordinary conversation should be a channel of grace. The verse thus sets the new man’s rule of speech: not only refraining from rotten talk, but speaking what edifies and blesses — words that minister grace. The tongue, like the hands of verse 28, is to be turned from harm to positive good for others.

So the witnesses see Paul regulate the speech of the new man. “Corrupt” — rotten, putrid, like decayed fruit — covers foul, obscene, useless, and harmful talk. None of this is to “proceed out of your mouth,” for filthy words spring from corruption in the speaker and corrupt the hearers (Henry). The high positive standard: speech “good to the use of edifying” — suited to the need — “that it may minister grace unto the hearers,” conveying real spiritual benefit. The test is not the absence of evil but the presence of good: ordinary conversation should be a channel of grace.
30:
On Ephesians 4:30 — “And grieve not the holy Spirit of God, whereby ye are sealed unto the day of redemption” — the witnesses mark the warning against grieving the Spirit.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the tenderness. Robertson: “grieve not” (lypeite), cease grieving; “ye were sealed” (Ephesians 1:13). Ellicott: more than “quenching” (1 Thessalonians 5:19) or “resisting” the Spirit. Adam Clarke: by any wrong temper or word. Wesley: “do not force him to withdraw… as a friend whom you grieve.”

The verse gives the deepest motive for holy speech and conduct. Do not grieve the Holy Spirit, by whom you are sealed for the day of redemption. The Spirit is a person who can be grieved.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the whole exhortation in reverence for the indwelling Spirit. The witnesses note the tender word “grieve” — the Holy Spirit is a person, not a mere influence, and can be made sorrowful, as a loving friend is grieved by unkind behaviour. They mark that the foregoing sins — lying, festering anger, corrupt speech — and those to follow are summed up as grieving the Spirit; this, Ellicott observes, is even more than “quenching” or “resisting” him — it is wounding the love of the divine Guest. They observe the precious motive attached: “whereby ye are sealed unto the day of redemption” — the same Spirit who is grieved by sin is the One who has sealed believers as God’s own, the guarantee and mark of their final redemption. They note the wonder: the very Spirit we grieve is the Spirit who secures our salvation; how then should we wound him? They observe Wesley’s warning not to force the grieved Spirit to withdraw. The verse thus supplies the tenderest and weightiest motive for the new man’s holiness: love and reverence for the indwelling, sealing Spirit, whom sin grieves. To walk worthily is, above all, not to grieve the Holy Guest who dwells within and guarantees our redemption.

So the witnesses see Paul ground the whole exhortation in reverence for the indwelling Spirit. The tender word “grieve” — the Holy Spirit is a person, not a mere influence, and can be made sorrowful, as a loving friend is grieved by unkind behaviour. The foregoing sins are summed up as grieving the Spirit; this, Ellicott observes, is more than “quenching” or “resisting” him. The precious motive: “whereby ye are sealed unto the day of redemption” — the very Spirit we grieve is the One who has sealed us as God’s own and secures our final redemption. How then should we wound him?
31:
On Ephesians 4:31 — “Let all bitterness, and wrath, and anger, and clamour, and evil speaking, be put away from you, with all malice” — the witnesses mark the catalogue of sins to be removed.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the list. Robertson: “bitterness” (pikria), “clamour” (krauge, outcry); “be put away” (arthето, pick up and carry off; cf. Colossians 3:8). The Reformed Calvin: he condemns anger with its companions, noisy disputes and reproaches. The Eastern Chrysostom: as bees shun an unclean vessel. Wesley distinguishes the gradations.

The verse sweeps away a cluster of related sins. Let all bitterness, wrath, anger, clamour, evil speaking, and malice be removed. The whole brood of angry, harsh tempers is to go.

So the witnesses see Paul gather up and banish the whole family of angry, malicious tempers. The witnesses note the catalogue, tracing a progression Wesley discerns: “bitterness” — the settled, sour resentment, the root; “wrath” and “anger” — the heated passion and its outbursts; “clamour” — the loud quarreling, brawling, and shouting in which anger vents itself; and “evil speaking” — the slander, railing, and reviling that wound others. They mark the comprehensive close: “with all malice” — the ill-will and desire to harm that underlies them all. They observe the decisive command: “be put away” — picked up and carried off entirely, like refuse removed from the house; these have no place in the new man. They note Chrysostom’s image that, as bees will not settle in an unclean vessel, so the Spirit will not dwell where such tempers are harboured. They observe that this is the negative counterpart to the positive graces of the next verse. The verse thus sweeps the heart clean of the whole brood of harsh, angry, malicious dispositions — clearing the ground for the kindness, tenderness, and forgiveness that are to take their place.

So the witnesses see Paul gather up and banish the whole family of angry, malicious tempers. The catalogue traces a progression (Wesley): “bitterness” (the settled, sour root), “wrath” and “anger” (the heated passion and its outbursts), “clamour” (loud brawling), and “evil speaking” (slander and reviling). The comprehensive close: “with all malice” — the ill-will underlying them all. The command “be put away” — carried off entirely, like refuse removed from the house. Chrysostom’s image: as bees will not settle in an unclean vessel, so the Spirit will not dwell where such tempers are harboured.
32:
On Ephesians 4:32 — “And be ye kind one to another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, even as God for Christ’s sake hath forgiven you” — the witnesses mark the graces that replace malice.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the positive virtues. Robertson: “be ye kind” (chrestoi, used of God, Romans 2:4); “tenderhearted” (eusplanchnoi, 1 Peter 3:8). Ellicott: kindness, tenderheartedness, free forgiveness “after the model of… God.” The Eastern Chrysostom: Paul founds the exhortation on what God has done in Christ. Wesley: God “kind and tenderhearted… hath forgiven you.”

The verse crowns the chapter with the graces that replace the banished sins. Be kind, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God forgave you in Christ. The divine forgiveness is the pattern and motive.

So the witnesses see Paul fill the swept heart with the positive graces, modelled on God himself. The witnesses note the contrast with verse 31: in place of bitterness and malice, “be ye kind one to another” — gentle, gracious, obliging, the word used of God’s own kindness; “tenderhearted” — compassionate, with hearts easily moved to pity and warmth toward others. They mark the crowning grace: “forgiving one another” — freely pardoning offenses, the direct opposite of the cherished anger and malice just condemned. They observe the great pattern and motive: “even as God for Christ’s sake hath forgiven you” — the believer’s forgiveness is to be measured by, and flow from, God’s own free, full forgiveness granted for Christ’s sake. They note Chrysostom’s point that Paul founds the exhortation on what God has already done in Christ, the strongest of all motives. They observe that to be forgiven so freely and at such cost lays on the believer the sweet obligation to forgive. The verse thus crowns the chapter’s ethic: the new man, having put away all malice, clothes himself with kindness, compassion, and forgiveness, taking the forgiving God in Christ as his pattern. So the worthy walk, begun in lowliness and unity, ends in the Christlike grace of free forgiveness.

So the witnesses see Paul fill the swept heart with positive graces, modelled on God himself. In place of bitterness and malice: “be ye kind one to another” — the word used of God’s own kindness; “tenderhearted” — compassionate, easily moved to pity; and the crowning grace, “forgiving one another,” the opposite of cherished malice. The great pattern and motive: “even as God for Christ’s sake hath forgiven you” — the believer’s forgiveness measured by and flowing from God’s own free pardon. Chrysostom’s point: Paul founds the exhortation on what God has done in Christ, the strongest of all motives.
